title: Judges: Chaos without a King
objective: Deepen biblical-theological understanding of judges: chaos without a king and its place in redemptive history.

required_passages.text: Selected key passages for this unit segment, read in full during lesson delivery.

segments.audio_script: Today we study judges: chaos without a king and trace God's covenant faithfulness.
Listen to the assigned passage and note covenant themes, divine actions, and human response.
We place this text in its historical setting, canonical location, and literary flow.
We analyze theological meaning, covenant development, and connections to the wider biblical story.
Key themes include God's holiness, covenant faithfulness, judgment and mercy, and redemptive purpose.

Review question: What statement is repeated twice at the end of Judges to explain the chaos described in chapters 17-21? Answer: In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
segments.correct_answer: In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
acceptable_variants: everyone did what was right in their own eyes — no king in israel
there was no king and everyone lived by their own standard
Judges ends with — no king, everyone doing what seemed right to them

segments.audio_script: Carry this lesson into prayer, worship, and careful Scripture reading as we continue the course.

vocabulary.definition: The state described at the end of Judges where absence of authoritative leadership produced social and religious chaos; it prepares the reader for the monarchy
The tribe in Judges 17-18 that stole a Levite and set up an idolatrous shrine in the north; their story illustrates how far Israel had fallen from covenant faithfulness
